result:
Sometimes I wonder if thoughts are more like constellations than sentences—tiny pinpricks of light that only form patterns when we step back far enough from ourselves. At eye level they feel jagged, non-linear: a grocery list tangled with the memory of your mother’s hands smoothing dough on a winter afternoon, the persistent blinking cursor on an email you can’t finish, the sudden synesthetic smell of copper when you remember the bridge you almost jumped from in 2009. You wake at 3:07 a.m. and discover an entire galaxy rotating inside your sternum. The heart, after all, was never a metronome; it is a messy, orchestral nebula expanding in quantum increments.

I read once that the universe is flat, or maybe it was only the cosmic microwave background that is flat, which somehow makes everything worse and better at the same time—like learning that infinity, if stretched far enough, behaves like printer paper. This is comforting until you realize paper cuts. I keep a tiny envelope of receipts in my desk drawer labeled “Unlikely Apostles.” Among them: a ticket stub from a movie I walked out of because the couple in front of me could not stop reminding each other that love is performative; a vending-machine sticky hand that reached too far for a Cheez-It and never came back; the fortune from a cookie cracked open the night my friend died—“A thrilling time is in your immediate future.” Maybe the universe is a joke with impeccable timing. Maybe grief is the punch line we rehearse in private until it no longer sounds like a sob.

There’s a kind of silence that happens when you hold a seashell to your ear in a room with no ocean. You hear the wash of your own blood, which oceanographers assure us is mostly the same salinity as primordial brine. Sometimes I think we are all just amphorae rattling with ancestral tides. My grandmother used to say that in her village every infant was given a stone from the river to suck on when they teethed, so that the taste of home was the first thing to cut the gums and the last thing to dissolve on the tongue. I lost my stone on a playground somewhere in the suburbs. Now all left of her legacy is a faint calcium memory clinging to my molars and the knowledge that somewhere a child-size sneaker lies fossilized against time like those pressed flowers people leave in hymnals and forget to reclaim.

Entropy endears me. I love how rust insists that even iron must eventually answer to softness. Last spring I watched a swing set swallowed whole by kudzu behind my old elementary school, the chains still swaying like pendulums measuring nonexistent playgrounds. It felt like witnessing a mercy killing: childhood mulched back into chlorophyll. On the walk home I noticed every street sign was tagged with a sticker that read “YOU WERE HERE BEFORE THEY NAMED THE ROADS.” A simple vandalism, yet it accomplished what centuries of maps never could—turning asphalt back into possibility. I peeled one off and stuck it to the back of my phone. Now every call begins and ends with being pre-named, like Adam standing before anything had syllables.

Muscle memory is an insurrectionist. It took me three months after my breakup to stop turning left at Maple and 4th toward her apartment; my car steered itself with restless loyalty. One night I finally made it to the river instead. I rolled down the windows and screamed the quadratic formula, just to prove there were still rational numbers in the world. A homeless man fishing at the bank applauded when I finished, then asked if I knew any lullabies. I sang him the one my mother hummed while defrosting chicken thighs, a minor-key rendition that made the herons hush. When I left, he offered me a sandwich crust and an ancient Walkman with one tape inside: side A blank, side B a shouted grocery list. I have never listened, waiting for the day I need instructions more than mystery.

In Japanese folklore, when an object reaches one hundred years of age it wakes into consciousness called tsukumogami. I wonder how my keyboard feels typing this—each clack a pulse it never asked for. Perhaps my coffee mug, chipped and inked with café logos from four different cities, resents having to hold the fluid that erodes its porcelain. Maybe it dreams in porcelain storms, longing to be a teacup again, dignified and delicate; instead she suffers my unwashed fingerprints like graffiti on her celestial ceiling. At night I set her in the sink with tender apology, as one might rinse a baptismal font that’s memorized the sins of too many congregants.

Yesterday the horizon wore a necklace of contrails, each jet closer than permissible in pandemic airspace. For a moment I mistook them for migrating birds practicing social distancing in reverse—crowding sky because the earth below grew wide and alien. Flights resumed, yet the pause remains echoing somewhere above the troposphere, like musical rest nobody can conduct. Airports now feel like museums devoted to last year’s prophecies. I saw a child licking a departure board just to taste the places. Father swatted him gently, apologized to thin air, then crouched and asked what flavor California was. The boy licked his lips and said, “Not enough salt.” I bought them both Cinnabon because sweetness seems like a currency still accepted across borders, across time, across mouths not yet taught to fear tongues.

We are told to keep emergency granola bars in our glove compartments for disasters, but nobody says what kind—hurricanes, heartbreaks, the sudden conviction you misplaced an entire decade behind the dry-cleaning ticket in your coat pocket. I stock mine with dark chocolate and annotated road atlases. Once, after a tornado watch expired harmlessly, I tore out the page for the state I’d just left, folded it into a paper boat, and launched it down the gutter. It caught briefly on a storm grate, spinning like a dog chasing its own void. Semi-truck exhaust hiccupped southern humidity over it until the ink bled, letters loosening off the page like fish scales.ales. I watched until the map was unreadable, which felt like the kind of closure cartographers deserve but rarely get.

The moon tonight is an overripe grapefruit wedged between two rooftops leaking lavender dusk. I think of the phrase “lunar hospitality”: offering the night just enough light to not die, but not enough to see the cliff before you step. Moths appreciate the austerity, thumping against porch bulbs like tiny kamikaze prayers. I used to swat them away, but now I understand their suicide as a literal chase for radiance; what else would you do if the closest you ever got to God was a 60-watt filament burning in witness? Somewhere Galileo’s finger is mummified in a museum, pointing upward, mostly toward gift shops selling fridge magnets of the very finger that once grazed truth. I, too, have pointed at things I barely understood—thermostats, ECG machines, my own reflection—pretending the gesture was enough.

My father kept a box labeled “Pieces of String Too Short to Use,” a Borges joke materialized in Rubbermaid. When he died we found rubber bands, receipts, a single baby tooth—mine—wrapped in a gum wrapper fortune: “Keep true to the dreams of thy youth.” String resists entropy longer than skin, I guess. I kept the tooth but moved the string to a mason jar. Sometimes I open it and let the pieces spill like tally marks nobody is left to count. Each fragment inches me closer to some asymptotic mercy, the way paper snowflakes approximate winter but never catch cold. There is comfort in the imperfect replica; it reminds me that mimesis is love’s modest admission that originals are too bright to hold.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
